items.q: Where does the main verb go in 'Ich kann gut Deutsch sprechen'?
items.correct: At the end of the clause (sprechen).
items.why: Students place the infinitive next to the modal. German modals push the main verb's INFINITIVE to the END: 'Ich kann gut Deutsch SPRECHEN' - the modal (kann) is in V2 position; the infinitive goes last.
Students assume gender follows meaning. German gender is grammatical, not biological. The diminutive suffix -CHEN makes any noun neuter, regardless of natural gender. So das Mädchen, das Mädel.

items.q: Which auxiliary does 'gehen' (to go) take in the perfect tense?
items.why: Students default to haben (English 'have'). Verbs of motion or change of state take SEIN (gehen, kommen, laufen, werden). Most other verbs take haben. 'Ich habe gegangen' is incorrect.

items.q: In what year was the UDHR adopted?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1948. “1945” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Chancengleichheit. “Lohngleichheit” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many articles in the UDHR?
items.why: Exact answer: 30. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
